For a compact overview of my academic background, see my Curriculum Vitae.

title: First Variation of Area
text: The first variation formula measures how the area of an immersed hypersurface changes under an infinitesimal deformation. Its Euler-Lagrange equation is the minimal-surface equation \(H=0\).

title: First Variation of Area
text:
Geometric Setup
Let \(F_t:\Sigma\to M\) be a smooth one-parameter variation of an immersion \(F=F_0\). Its variational vector field is
\[
X=\left.\frac{\partial F_t}{\partial t}\right|_{t=0}.
\]
Write \(g_t=F_t^*\bar g\) for the induced metric and \(d\mu_t\) for its volume form. Along \(\Sigma\), decompose
\[
X=X^\top+f\nu,
\]
where \(X^\top\) is tangential, \(\nu\) is a unit normal, and \(f=\langle X,\nu\rangle\) is the normal speed.

title: First Variation of Area
text:
Variation of the Metric
For tangent vectors \(e_i,e_j\) on \(\Sigma\),
\[
\left.\frac{d}{dt}\right|_{t=0}(g_t)_{ij}
=\langle \bar\nabla_{e_i}X,e_j\rangle
+\langle e_i,\bar\nabla_{e_j}X\rangle.
\]
Taking the trace and using \(\frac{d}{dt}d\mu_t=\frac12\operatorname{tr}_{g_t}(\dot g_t)d\mu_t\) gives
\[
\left.\frac{d}{dt}\right|_{t=0}d\mu_t
=\bigl(\operatorname{div}_\Sigma X^\top-Hf\bigr)d\mu.
\]
Here the sign convention is \(H=\operatorname{div}_\Sigma\nu\).

title: First Variation of Area
text:
First Variation Formula
Integrating the variation of the area form yields
\[
\left.\frac{d}{dt}\right|_{t=0}\operatorname{Area}(F_t(\Sigma))
=\int_\Sigma \operatorname{div}_\Sigma X^\top\,d\mu
-\int_\Sigma Hf\,d\mu.
\]
If \(\Sigma\) is closed, or if the variation is compactly supported away from \(\partial\Sigma\), the divergence term vanishes. Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\left.\frac{d}{dt}\right|_{t=0}\operatorname{Area}(F_t(\Sigma))
=-\int_\Sigma Hf\,d\mu
}
\]

title: First Variation of Area
text:
Minimality
A hypersurface is stationary for every compactly supported normal variation precisely when
\[
H=0.
\]
Thus minimal hypersurfaces are exactly the critical points of the area functional. This is the geometric analogue of the Euler-Lagrange equation in the calculus of variations.






NoteSign convention



Some authors define the mean curvature with the opposite sign. Under that convention the right-hand side of the first variation formula changes sign, but the stationarity condition remains \(H=0\).

title: Xingjian Zhou
text:
I am a Ph.D. student in mathematics at Xiamen University and a joint Ph.D. student at the University of Trento. My research lies in geometric analysis, especially geometric inequalities and monotonicity formulas arising from elliptic partial differential equations.
This website collects my research, publications, curriculum vitae, and mathematical notes.

Read my notes View publications

title: Xingjian Zhou

Research
My current work focuses on scalar curvature problems, rigidity phenomena, and analytic aspects of the Penrose inequality. See the dedicated Publications page for my peer-reviewed articles and current preprints.

title: Curriculum Vitae
text:
Research Interests
My research lies in geometric analysis, focusing on monotonicity formulas and geometric inequalities associated with elliptic equations arising in differential geometry. Current interests include scalar curvature problems, rigidity phenomena, and analytic aspects of the Penrose inequality.
Keywords: Potential theory; nonlinear elliptic and parabolic PDEs; inverse mean curvature flow; minimal surfaces; scalar curvature.

title: Conformal Changes of Riemannian Metrics
text:
\(\newcommand{\owedge}{\mathbin{\rlap{\bigcirc}\mkern5mu\lower0.02ex{\scriptstyle\wedge}\mkern3mu}}\newcommand{\II}{\mathrm{I\!I}}\)
Open PDF version
We assume that the reader is familiar with the basic notions of smooth manifolds, Riemannian manifolds, Riemannian metrics, and Levi–Civita connections.

title: Conformal Changes of Riemannian Metrics
text:
Introduction
We study the transformation laws for geometric quantities under a conformal change of metric. Let \((M^n,g)\) be an \(n\)-dimensional Riemannian manifold, and consider the conformally related metric
\[
\widetilde g=e^{2f}g,
\]
where \(f\in C^\infty(M)\).
The Levi–Civita connection, Riemann curvature tensor, Ricci curvature tensor, and scalar curvature associated with \(g\) will be denoted by
\[
\nabla,
\qquad
\mathrm{Rm},
\qquad
\mathrm{Ric},
\qquad
\mathrm{Sc},
\]
respectively. The corresponding quantities with respect to \(\widetilde g\) will be denoted by
\[
\widetilde{\nabla},
\qquad
\widetilde{\mathrm{Rm}},
\qquad
\widetilde{\mathrm{Ric}},
\qquad
\widetilde{\mathrm{Sc}}.
\]

title: Conformal Changes of Riemannian Metrics
text:
Sign Conventions
Our convention for the Riemann curvature tensor is
\[
\mathrm{Rm}(X,Y)Z
=
\nabla_Y\nabla_XZ
-
\nabla_X\nabla_YZ
+
\nabla_{[X,Y]}Z.
\]
The Ricci curvature tensor is defined by
\[
\mathrm{Ric}(X,Y)
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
g\bigl(\mathrm{Rm}(X,e_i)Y,e_i\bigr),
\]
where \(\{e_i\}_{i=1}^n\) is a local \(g\)-orthonormal frame.
The scalar curvature is defined by
\[
\mathrm{Sc}
=
\operatorname{tr}_g\mathrm{Ric}
=
\sum_{i=1}^n\mathrm{Ric}(e_i,e_i).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\mathrm{Sc}
=
\sum_{i,j=1}^n
g\bigl(\mathrm{Rm}(e_i,e_j)e_i,e_j\bigr).
\]
With these conventions, a Riemannian manifold of constant sectional curvature \(K\) satisfies
\[
\mathrm{Ric}=(n-1)Kg,
\qquad
\mathrm{Sc}=n(n-1)K.
\]
For a smooth function \(f\), its Hessian is defined by
\[
\nabla^2f(X,Y)
=
g(\nabla_X\nabla f,Y),
\]
and the Laplace–Beltrami operator is defined by
\[
\Delta f
=
\operatorname{tr}_g(\nabla^2f).
\]

title: Conformal Changes of Riemannian Metrics
text:
Hypersurface Conventions
Let \(\Sigma^{n-1}\subset M^n\) be an immersed hypersurface. With respect to \(g\), denote its unit normal, second fundamental form, and mean curvature by
\[
\nu,
\qquad
\II,
\qquad
H,
\]
respectively.
We adopt the convention
\[
\II(X,Y)
=
g(\nabla_X\nu,Y),
\qquad
X,Y\in T\Sigma,
\]
and define the mean curvature by
\[
H
=
\operatorname{tr}_{g_\Sigma}\II,
\]
where \(g_\Sigma\) is the metric induced on \(\Sigma\) by \(g\). Thus, \(H\) is the trace of the second fundamental form, rather than its normalized average.
The corresponding quantities computed with respect to \(\widetilde g\) will be denoted by
\[
\widetilde{\nu},
\qquad
\widetilde{\II},
\qquad
\widetilde H.
\]
Our goal is to derive the transformation laws for these intrinsic and extrinsic geometric quantities under the conformal change
\[
g\longmapsto\widetilde g=e^{2f}g.
\]

title: Conformal Changes of Riemannian Metrics
text:
Conformal Transformation Formulas






NoteKulkarni–Nomizu product



Let \(P\) and \(Q\) be symmetric \((0,2)\)-tensors on \(M\). Their Kulkarni–Nomizu product is the \((0,4)\)-tensor \(P\owedge Q\) defined by
\[
\begin{aligned}
(P\owedge Q)(X,Y,Z,W)
={}&P(X,Z)Q(Y,W)+P(Y,W)Q(X,Z)\\
&-P(X,W)Q(Y,Z)-P(Y,Z)Q(X,W).
\end{aligned}
\]


We regard the Riemann curvature tensor as a \((0,4)\)-tensor by setting
\[
\mathrm{Rm}(X,Y,Z,W)
=
g\bigl(\mathrm{Rm}(X,Y)Z,W\bigr).
\]
For any smooth vector fields \(X,Y\) on \(M\), the Levi–Civita connections of \(g\) and \(\widetilde g\) satisfy
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\nabla}_XY
=
\nabla_XY+X(f)Y+Y(f)X-g(X,Y)\nabla f.
}
\]
Regarding the Riemann curvature tensors as \((0,4)\)-tensors, one has
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\mathrm{Rm}}
=
e^{2f}
\left[
\mathrm{Rm}
-
g\owedge
\left(
\nabla^2f-df\otimes df+\frac12|\nabla f|_g^2g
\right)
\right].
}
\]
Equivalently, for any smooth vector fields \(X,Y,Z,W\) on \(M\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\widetilde{\mathrm{Rm}}(X,Y,Z,W)
=e^{2f}\Bigl\{
&\mathrm{Rm}(X,Y,Z,W)\\
&-g(X,Z)\nabla^2f(Y,W)-g(Y,W)\nabla^2f(X,Z)\\
&+g(X,W)\nabla^2f(Y,Z)+g(Y,Z)\nabla^2f(X,W)\\
&+g(X,Z)Y(f)W(f)+g(Y,W)X(f)Z(f)\\
&-g(X,W)Y(f)Z(f)-g(Y,Z)X(f)W(f)\\
&-|\nabla f|_g^2
\bigl(g(X,Z)g(Y,W)-g(X,W)g(Y,Z)\bigr)
\Bigr\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
The Ricci curvature tensors satisfy
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\mathrm{Ric}}
=
\mathrm{Ric}
-(n-2)
\left(
\nabla^2f-df\otimes df+\frac12|\nabla f|_g^2g
\right)
-
\left(
\Delta f+\frac{n-2}{2}|\nabla f|_g^2
\right)g.
}
\]
The scalar curvatures satisfy
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\mathrm{Sc}}
=
e^{-2f}
\left[
\mathrm{Sc}
-2(n-1)
\left(
\Delta f+\frac{n-2}{2}|\nabla f|_g^2
\right)
\right].
}
\]
Let \(\Sigma^{n-1}\subset M^n\) be an immersed hypersurface. Then
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\nu}=e^{-f}\nu.
}
\]
For all \(X,Y\in T\Sigma\), the second fundamental forms satisfy
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\II}(X,Y)
=
e^f\left[\II(X,Y)+\nu(f)g(X,Y)\right].
}
\]
Finally, the mean curvatures satisfy
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde H
=
e^{-f}\left[H+(n-1)\nu(f)\right].
}
\]
